title: UiAutomator2 Driver
overview: UiAutomator2 is the default Android automation driver in Appium 2, bootstrapping a test server APK on the device that processes commands.
whyItMatters: Understanding the driver architecture helps troubleshoot connection issues, app installation failures, and explains the limits of Android automation.
explanation: When an Appium session starts with `automationName: UiAutomator2`, Appium installs a helper APK (`appium-uiautomator2-server`) onto the device. This server uses Google's native UIAutomator V2 framework to interact with the device. It supports deep OS integration: changing network settings, reading notifications, accessing the clipboard, and sending SMS via adb. It replaced the older Appium Android Driver.
keyPoints: Requires Appium to install two helper APKs on the device.
Can interact with system apps (Settings, Notifications) unlike Espresso.
realWorldExample: To verify an OTP sent via SMS, an SDET uses the UiAutomator2 driver to open the notification shade (`openNotifications()`), read the text from the message, and then switch back to the app to input it.
commonMistakes: Not uninstalling the `appium-uiautomator2-server` APKs when troubleshooting weird session creation errors.
Expecting it to interact with web views easily (context switching is required).
bestPractices: Use `appium driver update uiautomator2` via CLI to keep the driver up to date in Appium 2.
Use `adb` commands alongside the driver when necessary for deep OS control.

interviewQuestions.q: How does Appium interact with an Android device?
interviewQuestions.a: Appium installs the UiAutomator2 server APK onto the device. The Appium server sends REST commands to this APK, which translates them into native Google UIAutomator API calls.

revisionSummary: UiAutomator2 is the engine for Android automation in Appium. It operates via a helper APK on the device, providing access to both the AUT and system-level features like notifications and the clipboard.
